texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: GLOSSARY
texts.text: GLOSSARY

texts.label: text
texts.orig: abandon/abandonment -the tenant's remedy of moving out of a rental unit that is uninhabitable and that the landlord has not repaired within a reasonable time after receiving notice of the defects from the tenant.
texts.text: abandon/abandonment -the tenant's remedy of moving out of a rental unit that is uninhabitable and that the landlord has not repaired within a reasonable time after receiving notice of the defects from the tenant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: amount of notice/amount of advance notice -the number of days' notice that must be given before a change in the tenancy can take effect. Usually, the amount of advance notice is the same as the number of days between rent payments. For example, in a month-to-month tenancy, the landlord usually must give the tenant 30 days' advance written notice that the landlord is increasing the amount of the security deposit.
texts.text: amount of notice/amount of advance notice -the number of days' notice that must be given before a change in the tenancy can take effect. Usually, the amount of advance notice is the same as the number of days between rent payments. For example, in a month-to-month tenancy, the landlord usually must give the tenant 30 days' advance written notice that the landlord is increasing the amount of the security deposit.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: appeal -a request to a higher court to review a lower court's decision in a lawsuit.
texts.text: appeal -a request to a higher court to review a lower court's decision in a lawsuit.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Application for Waiver of Court Fees and Costs -a form that tenants may complete and give to the Clerk of Court to request permission to le court documents without paying the court ling fee.
texts.text: Application for Waiver of Court Fees and Costs -a form that tenants may complete and give to the Clerk of Court to request permission to le court documents without paying the court ling fee.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: arbitration -using a neutral third person to resolve a dispute instead of going to court. Arbitration results can be binding or non-binding on the parties. A binding result means the result is nal without an opportunity to appeal or contest it and the prevailing party can enforce it (like a judgment) against the non-prevailing party. A non-binding result means a determination that is not binding on the parties, meaning that either party can still le a legal action against the other party in the Superior Court.
texts.text: arbitration -using a neutral third person to resolve a dispute instead of going to court. Arbitration results can be binding or non-binding on the parties. A binding result means the result is nal without an opportunity to appeal or contest it and the prevailing party can enforce it (like a judgment) against the non-prevailing party. A non-binding result means a determination that is not binding on the parties, meaning that either party can still le a legal action against the other party in the Superior Court.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: arbitrator -a neutral third person, agreed to by the parties in a dispute, who hears and decides a dispute (see arbitration; compare to mediator).
texts.text: arbitrator -a neutral third person, agreed to by the parties in a dispute, who hears and decides a dispute (see arbitration; compare to mediator).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: assign/assignment -an agreement between the original tenant and a new tenant by which the new tenant takes over the rental agreement pertaining to the unit and becomes responsible to the landlord for everything that the original tenant was responsible for. The original tenant is still responsible to the landlord if the new tenant does not live up to the obligations of the rental agreement (see novation; compare to sublease).
texts.text: assign/assignment -an agreement between the original tenant and a new tenant by which the new tenant takes over the rental agreement pertaining to the unit and becomes responsible to the landlord for everything that the original tenant was responsible for. The original tenant is still responsible to the landlord if the new tenant does not live up to the obligations of the rental agreement (see novation; compare to sublease).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: California Civil Rights Department -the state agency that investigates complaints of unlawful discrimination in housing and employment.
texts.text: California Civil Rights Department -the state agency that investigates complaints of unlawful discrimination in housing and employment.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Claim of Right to Possession -a form that the occupants of a rental unit can ll out to temporarily stop their eviction by the sheriff after the landlord has won an unlawful detainer (eviction) lawsuit. The occupants can use this form only if: the landlord did not serve a Prejudgment Claim of Right to Possession form with the summons and complaint; the occupants were not named in the writ of possession; and the occupants have lived in the rental unit since before the unlawful detainer lawsuit was led.
texts.text: Claim of Right to Possession -a form that the occupants of a rental unit can ll out to temporarily stop their eviction by the sheriff after the landlord has won an unlawful detainer (eviction) lawsuit. The occupants can use this form only if: the landlord did not serve a Prejudgment Claim of Right to Possession form with the summons and complaint; the occupants were not named in the writ of possession; and the occupants have lived in the rental unit since before the unlawful detainer lawsuit was led.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: COVID-19 Tenant Relief Act of 2020 (the Tenant Relief Act) -An emergency act that took effect on August 31, 2020, which (1) placed a moratorium on evictions through September 30, 2021, for a tenant's failure to pay rent due to nancial distress arising from or related to COVID-19, provided the tenant returned to the landlord a signed declaration of COVID-19-Related Financial Distress and (2) provided tenants and landlords nancial assistance for unpaid rent and utilities through March 31, 2022. For more information about the Tenant Relief Act, please go to https://housing.ca.gov/.
texts.text: COVID-19 Tenant Relief Act of 2020 (the Tenant Relief Act) -An emergency act that took effect on August 31, 2020, which (1) placed a moratorium on evictions through September 30, 2021, for a tenant's failure to pay rent due to nancial distress arising from or related to COVID-19, provided the tenant returned to the landlord a signed declaration of COVID-19-Related Financial Distress and (2) provided tenants and landlords nancial assistance for unpaid rent and utilities through March 31, 2022. For more information about the Tenant Relief Act, please go to https://housing.ca.gov/.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: credit report -a report prepared by a credit reporting agency that describes a person's credit history for the last seven years (except for bankruptcies, which are reported for 10 years). A credit report shows, for example, whether the person pays his or her bills on time, has delinquent or charged-off accounts.
texts.text: credit report -a report prepared by a credit reporting agency that describes a person's credit history for the last seven years (except for bankruptcies, which are reported for 10 years). A credit report shows, for example, whether the person pays his or her bills on time, has delinquent or charged-off accounts.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: credit reporting agency -a business that keeps records of people's credit histories, and that reports credit history information to prospective creditors (including landlords) (see tenant screening service).
texts.text: credit reporting agency -a business that keeps records of people's credit histories, and that reports credit history information to prospective creditors (including landlords) (see tenant screening service).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: credit score -a numerical summary of a person's credit worthiness that is based on information from a credit reporting agency . Credit scoring uses a statistical program to compare a person's history of bill paying, credit accounts, collection actions and other credit information with the credit performance of other consumers. A high credit score (for example, 750 and up) indicates a history of better credit performance than other consumers, and potentially a better credit risk. A low credit score (for example, 300-400) indicates a history of worse credit performance than other consumers, and potentially a worse credit risk.
texts.text: credit score -a numerical summary of a person's credit worthiness that is based on information from a credit reporting agency . Credit scoring uses a statistical program to compare a person's history of bill paying, credit accounts, collection actions and other credit information with the credit performance of other consumers. A high credit score (for example, 750 and up) indicates a history of better credit performance than other consumers, and potentially a better credit risk. A low credit score (for example, 300-400) indicates a history of worse credit performance than other consumers, and potentially a worse credit risk.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: default judgment -a judgment issued by the court, without a hearing, after the defendant has failed to le a response to the plaintiff's complaint.
texts.text: default judgment -a judgment issued by the court, without a hearing, after the defendant has failed to le a response to the plaintiff's complaint.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: demurrer -a legal response that a defendant can le in a lawsuit to test the legal suciency of the charges made in the plaintiff's complaint. In an unlawful detainer action to evict a tenant, the tenant may le a demurrer to the landlord's complaint if the complaint is legally Insucient, for instance, the complaint fails to state that the landlord served the tenant with a 3-day pay or quit notice, 30-day notice, 60-day notice, or 90-day notice.
texts.text: demurrer -a legal response that a defendant can le in a lawsuit to test the legal suciency of the charges made in the plaintiff's complaint. In an unlawful detainer action to evict a tenant, the tenant may le a demurrer to the landlord's complaint if the complaint is legally Insucient, for instance, the complaint fails to state that the landlord served the tenant with a 3-day pay or quit notice, 30-day notice, 60-day notice, or 90-day notice.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: discovery -the process through which parties to a legal action are allowed to obtain relevant information known to other parties or non-parties before trial.
texts.text: discovery -the process through which parties to a legal action are allowed to obtain relevant information known to other parties or non-parties before trial.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: discrimination (in rental housing) -denying a person housing, telling a person that housing is not available (when the housing is actually available at that time), providing housing under inferior terms, providing unequal access to housing, harassing a person in connection with housing accommodations, or providing segregated housing because of a person's race, color, ancestry, national origin, citizenship, immigration status, primary language, age, religion, mental or physical disability, sex, gender, sexual orientation, gender identity, gender expression, genetic information, marital status, familial status, source of income, and/or military or veteran status. Discrimination also can be refusal to make reasonable accommodation or not allow a reasonable modication of the property in order for a person with a disability to be able to use and enjoy the property.
texts.text: discrimination (in rental housing) -denying a person housing, telling a person that housing is not available (when the housing is actually available at that time), providing housing under inferior terms, providing unequal access to housing, harassing a person in connection with housing accommodations, or providing segregated housing because of a person's race, color, ancestry, national origin, citizenship, immigration status, primary language, age, religion, mental or physical disability, sex, gender, sexual orientation, gender identity, gender expression, genetic information, marital status, familial status, source of income, and/or military or veteran status. Discrimination also can be refusal to make reasonable accommodation or not allow a reasonable modication of the property in order for a person with a disability to be able to use and enjoy the property.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: dishonored check -a check that the bank returns to the payee (the person who received the check) without paying it. The bank may return the check because the payor's (the check writer's) account did not have enough money to cover the check. This is called a 'bounced' or 'NSF' check. Or, the bank may return the check because the payor stopped payment on it.
texts.text: dishonored check -a check that the bank returns to the payee (the person who received the check) without paying it. The bank may return the check because the payor's (the check writer's) account did not have enough money to cover the check. This is called a 'bounced' or 'NSF' check. Or, the bank may return the check because the payor stopped payment on it.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: domestic violence -abuse perpetrated against a spouse, former spouse, cohabitant, former cohabitant, a person that the abuser is having or has a dating or engagement relationship, or a person that the abuser has had a child with. For more information regarding domestic violence, please see page 63.
texts.text: domestic violence -abuse perpetrated against a spouse, former spouse, cohabitant, former cohabitant, a person that the abuser is having or has a dating or engagement relationship, or a person that the abuser has had a child with. For more information regarding domestic violence, please see page 63.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: elder abuse -physical abuse, neglect, abandonment, isolation, abduction, or other treatment with resulting physical harm or pain or mental suffering against an elder or dependent adult.
texts.text: elder abuse -physical abuse, neglect, abandonment, isolation, abduction, or other treatment with resulting physical harm or pain or mental suffering against an elder or dependent adult.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: escrow account -a bank account into which a party deposits money, which the escrow ocer will disburse pursuant to the terms and conditions of the escrow agreement. If a rental unit is uninhabitable, the tenant may deposit withheld rent into an escrow account to be withdrawn and disbursed only when the landlord has corrected the uninhabitable conditions in the rental unit or when the tenant is ordered by a court to pay the withheld rent to the landlord.
texts.text: escrow account -a bank account into which a party deposits money, which the escrow ocer will disburse pursuant to the terms and conditions of the escrow agreement. If a rental unit is uninhabitable, the tenant may deposit withheld rent into an escrow account to be withdrawn and disbursed only when the landlord has corrected the uninhabitable conditions in the rental unit or when the tenant is ordered by a court to pay the withheld rent to the landlord.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: eviction -a court-administered proceeding for removing a tenant from a rental unit because the tenant has violated the rental agreement or did not comply with a notice ending the tenancy (also called an 'unlawful detainer' lawsuit).
texts.text: eviction -a court-administered proceeding for removing a tenant from a rental unit because the tenant has violated the rental agreement or did not comply with a notice ending the tenancy (also called an 'unlawful detainer' lawsuit).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: eviction moratorium -a temporary halt to eviction proceedings.
texts.text: eviction moratorium -a temporary halt to eviction proceedings.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: eviction notice -a notice intended to terminate a tenancy. An eviction notice can be a three-day notice (curable and incurable), 30-day notice, 60-day notice, or 90-day notice.
texts.text: eviction notice -a notice intended to terminate a tenancy. An eviction notice can be a three-day notice (curable and incurable), 30-day notice, 60-day notice, or 90-day notice.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: eviction process -the ocial process that the landlord follows to evict a tenant from a rental unit.
texts.text: eviction process -the ocial process that the landlord follows to evict a tenant from a rental unit.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: ex parte hearing -a way in which a party to a legal proceeding can bring a matter before a judge without an absolute requirement that all other parties be present. The party seeking the hearing has to inform the other parties that they are seeking the hearing and give them the opportunity to attend if they wish.
texts.text: ex parte hearing -a way in which a party to a legal proceeding can bring a matter before a judge without an absolute requirement that all other parties be present. The party seeking the hearing has to inform the other parties that they are seeking the hearing and give them the opportunity to attend if they wish.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: fair housing organizations -city, county or private organizations that help renters resolve housing discrimination problems.
texts.text: fair housing organizations -city, county or private organizations that help renters resolve housing discrimination problems.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: family childcare home -a family child care home is a 'home that regularly provides care, protection, and supervision for 14 or fewer children, in the [child care] provider's own home, for periods of less than 24 hours a day, while the parents or guardians are away.'[482 ] A family childcare home can be located in a single family home, apartment, condominium, townhome, duplex, and other multi-family buildings. Family childcare homes are licensed and regulated by the California Department of Social Services (Community Care Licensing Division) and California State Fire Marshal. Landlords have no authority to regulate family childcare homes. Family childcare providers are vital to the community by supporting the needs of children, parents and employers, and boosting economic development.
texts.text: family childcare home -a family child care home is a 'home that regularly provides care, protection, and supervision for 14 or fewer children, in the [child care] provider's own home, for periods of less than 24 hours a day, while the parents or guardians are away.'[482 ] A family childcare home can be located in a single family home, apartment, condominium, townhome, duplex, and other multi-family buildings. Family childcare homes are licensed and regulated by the California Department of Social Services (Community Care Licensing Division) and California State Fire Marshal. Landlords have no authority to regulate family childcare homes. Family childcare providers are vital to the community by supporting the needs of children, parents and employers, and boosting economic development.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: federal stay (or automatic stay) -an order of a federal bankruptcy court that temporarily stops proceedings in a state court, including an eviction proceeding.
texts.text: federal stay (or automatic stay) -an order of a federal bankruptcy court that temporarily stops proceedings in a state court, including an eviction proceeding.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: xed term rental agreement -a rental agreement between the landlord and tenant usually in writing, that establishes all the terms of the agreement and that lasts for a predetermined length of time with dened start and end dates (for example, six months or one year). Compare to periodic rental agreement.
texts.text: xed term rental agreement -a rental agreement between the landlord and tenant usually in writing, that establishes all the terms of the agreement and that lasts for a predetermined length of time with dened start and end dates (for example, six months or one year). Compare to periodic rental agreement.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: guest -a person who does not have the rights of a tenant, such as a person who stays in a transient hotel for fewer than seven days or someone staying at a rental unit at the invitation of the tenant.
texts.text: guest -a person who does not have the rights of a tenant, such as a person who stays in a transient hotel for fewer than seven days or someone staying at a rental unit at the invitation of the tenant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: habitable -a rental unit that is t for human beings to live in. A rental unit that substantially complies with building and safety code standards that materially affect tenants' health and safety is said to be 'habitable.' See uninhabitable and implied warranty of habitability.
texts.text: habitable -a rental unit that is t for human beings to live in. A rental unit that substantially complies with building and safety code standards that materially affect tenants' health and safety is said to be 'habitable.' See uninhabitable and implied warranty of habitability.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: holding deposit -a deposit that a tenant gives to a landlord to hold a rental unit while the landlord's approval or disapproval of the tenant's application is pending or until the tenant pays the rst month's rent and the security deposit.
texts.text: holding deposit -a deposit that a tenant gives to a landlord to hold a rental unit while the landlord's approval or disapproval of the tenant's application is pending or until the tenant pays the rst month's rent and the security deposit.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: implied warranty of habitability -a legal rule that requires landlords to maintain their rental units in a condition t for human beings to live in. This warranty applies to every single residential tenancy in California, no matter what. A rental unit must substantially comply with building and housing code standards that materially affect tenants' health and safety. The basic minimum requirements for a rental unit to be habitable are listed on pages 47-51.
texts.text: implied warranty of habitability -a legal rule that requires landlords to maintain their rental units in a condition t for human beings to live in. This warranty applies to every single residential tenancy in California, no matter what. A rental unit must substantially comply with building and housing code standards that materially affect tenants' health and safety. The basic minimum requirements for a rental unit to be habitable are listed on pages 47-51.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: initial inspection -an inspection by the landlord before the tenancy ends to identify defective conditions that justify deductions from the security deposit.
texts.text: initial inspection -an inspection by the landlord before the tenancy ends to identify defective conditions that justify deductions from the security deposit.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: just cause evictions -allowable grounds upon which a landlord may evict a tenant from a rental unit, including but not limited to, a tenant's nonpayment of rent or causing intentional damage to the rental unit.
texts.text: just cause evictions -allowable grounds upon which a landlord may evict a tenant from a rental unit, including but not limited to, a tenant's nonpayment of rent or causing intentional damage to the rental unit.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: landlord -a business or person who owns a rental unit, and who rents the unit to another person, called a tenant.
texts.text: landlord -a business or person who owns a rental unit, and who rents the unit to another person, called a tenant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: legal aid organizations -organizations that provide free legal advice, representation, and other legal services in noncriminal cases to economically disadvantaged persons.
texts.text: legal aid organizations -organizations that provide free legal advice, representation, and other legal services in noncriminal cases to economically disadvantaged persons.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: lockout -when a landlord locks a tenant out of the rental unit with the intent of terminating the tenancy. Lockouts, and all other self-help eviction remedies, are illegal. The term can also apply to what happens when a sheriff executes a writ of possession following a court eviction judgment.
texts.text: lockout -when a landlord locks a tenant out of the rental unit with the intent of terminating the tenancy. Lockouts, and all other self-help eviction remedies, are illegal. The term can also apply to what happens when a sheriff executes a writ of possession following a court eviction judgment.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: lodger -a person who lives in a room in a house where the owner lives. The owner may enter all areas occupied by the lodger and has overall control of the house.
texts.text: lodger -a person who lives in a room in a house where the owner lives. The owner may enter all areas occupied by the lodger and has overall control of the house.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: mediation -a process in which a neutral third person meets with the parties to a dispute in order to assist them in formulating a voluntary solution to the dispute.
texts.text: mediation -a process in which a neutral third person meets with the parties to a dispute in order to assist them in formulating a voluntary solution to the dispute.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: mediator -a neutral third person, agreed to by the parties to a dispute, who meets with the parties in order to assist them in formulating a voluntary solution to the dispute.
texts.text: mediator -a neutral third person, agreed to by the parties to a dispute, who meets with the parties in order to assist them in formulating a voluntary solution to the dispute.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The mediator's decision normally is not 'binding' on the parties (see mediation; compare to arbitrator).
texts.text: The mediator's decision normally is not 'binding' on the parties (see mediation; compare to arbitrator).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Memorandum to Set Case for Trial -a court document led in an unlawful detainer lawsuit requesting that the case be set for trial. This document also states whether the plaintiff (the landlord) has requested a jury trial. A tenant may also le such a document, it is then called a Counter-Memorandum.
texts.text: Memorandum to Set Case for Trial -a court document led in an unlawful detainer lawsuit requesting that the case be set for trial. This document also states whether the plaintiff (the landlord) has requested a jury trial. A tenant may also le such a document, it is then called a Counter-Memorandum.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Motion to Quash Service of Summons -a legal response that a defendant can le in a lawsuit if the defendant believes that plaintiff's service of the summons is defective. In an unlawful detainer action, rather than le an answer to the complaint, the defendant/tenant can le a motion to quash service of summons if he/she believes the landlord/plaintiff did not properly serve the summons and complaint.
texts.text: Motion to Quash Service of Summons -a legal response that a defendant can le in a lawsuit if the defendant believes that plaintiff's service of the summons is defective. In an unlawful detainer action, rather than le an answer to the complaint, the defendant/tenant can le a motion to quash service of summons if he/she believes the landlord/plaintiff did not properly serve the summons and complaint.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: negligence -a person's carelessness (that is, failure to use ordinary or reasonable care) that results in injury to another person or damage to another person's property.
texts.text: negligence -a person's carelessness (that is, failure to use ordinary or reasonable care) that results in injury to another person or damage to another person's property.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: notice to vacate -a notice intended to terminate a tenancy. A notice to vacate can be a 30-day notice, 60-day notice, or 90-day notice.
texts.text: notice to vacate -a notice intended to terminate a tenancy. A notice to vacate can be a 30-day notice, 60-day notice, or 90-day notice.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: novation -in an assignment situation, a novation is an agreement by the landlord, the original tenant, and the new tenant that makes the new tenant (rather than the original tenant) solely responsible to the landlord.
texts.text: novation -in an assignment situation, a novation is an agreement by the landlord, the original tenant, and the new tenant that makes the new tenant (rather than the original tenant) solely responsible to the landlord.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: occupant -a person who is not named as a tenant in the rental agreement who has moved into a rental unit before the landlord les an unlawful detainer (eviction) lawsuit. An occupant may be authorized by the rental agreement. If the landlord does not know that the occupant is living in the rental unit, the landlord may not name the occupant as a defendant in the unlawful detainer lawsuit.
texts.text: occupant -a person who is not named as a tenant in the rental agreement who has moved into a rental unit before the landlord les an unlawful detainer (eviction) lawsuit. An occupant may be authorized by the rental agreement. If the landlord does not know that the occupant is living in the rental unit, the landlord may not name the occupant as a defendant in the unlawful detainer lawsuit.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: periodic tenancy or tenancies -is a tenancy that continues week-to-week or monthto-month with no specied end date. Periodic tenancies continue from period to period until the landlord or tenant gives the other party notication that he/she wants to end the tenancy.
texts.text: periodic tenancy or tenancies -is a tenancy that continues week-to-week or monthto-month with no specied end date. Periodic tenancies continue from period to period until the landlord or tenant gives the other party notication that he/she wants to end the tenancy.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: periodic rental agreement -are tenancies that continue for successive periods until the landlord or tenant gives the other party notication that they want to end the tenancy. Examples of periodic tenancies are tenancies that run from week to week or month to month.
texts.text: periodic rental agreement -are tenancies that continue for successive periods until the landlord or tenant gives the other party notication that they want to end the tenancy. Examples of periodic tenancies are tenancies that run from week to week or month to month.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Prejudgment Claim of Right to Possession -a form that a landlord in an unlawful detainer (eviction) lawsuit can have served along with the summons and complaint on all persons living in the rental unit who might claim to be tenants, but whose names the landlord does not know. Occupants who are not named in the unlawful detainer complaint, but who claim a right to possess the rental unit, can ll out and le this form to become parties to the unlawful detainer action. This gives the occupant an opportunity to le an answer, but also exposes them to adverse consequences of an unlawful detainer, including a judgment against them. See Appendix 1 (122-123).
texts.text: Prejudgment Claim of Right to Possession -a form that a landlord in an unlawful detainer (eviction) lawsuit can have served along with the summons and complaint on all persons living in the rental unit who might claim to be tenants, but whose names the landlord does not know. Occupants who are not named in the unlawful detainer complaint, but who claim a right to possess the rental unit, can ll out and le this form to become parties to the unlawful detainer action. This gives the occupant an opportunity to le an answer, but also exposes them to adverse consequences of an unlawful detainer, including a judgment against them. See Appendix 1 (122-123).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: prepaid rental listing services -businesses that sell lists of available rental units.
texts.text: prepaid rental listing services -businesses that sell lists of available rental units.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: property manager -the property manager or rental agent is compensated by the landlord to represent the landlord's interests. In some instances, the tenant will deal with
texts.text: property manager -the property manager or rental agent is compensated by the landlord to represent the landlord's interests. In some instances, the tenant will deal with

texts.label: text
texts.orig: the rental agent or property manager on behalf of the landlord. In other instances, the tenant will deal directly with the landlord.
texts.text: the rental agent or property manager on behalf of the landlord. In other instances, the tenant will deal directly with the landlord.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: relief from forfeiture -an order by a court in an unlawful detainer (eviction) lawsuit that allows the losing tenant to remain in the rental unit, based on the tenant convincing the court that the eviction would cause the tenant severe hardship and that the tenant can pay all of the rent that is due, or otherwise fully comply with the terms of the rental agreement.
texts.text: relief from forfeiture -an order by a court in an unlawful detainer (eviction) lawsuit that allows the losing tenant to remain in the rental unit, based on the tenant convincing the court that the eviction would cause the tenant severe hardship and that the tenant can pay all of the rent that is due, or otherwise fully comply with the terms of the rental agreement.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: rent control -a government program that places a limit on the amount that a landlord can demand for renting a home or for renewing a rental agreement.
texts.text: rent control -a government program that places a limit on the amount that a landlord can demand for renting a home or for renewing a rental agreement.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: rent stabilization (or control) ordinances -laws in some communities that limit or prohibit rent increases, or that limit the circumstances in which a tenant can be evicted.
texts.text: rent stabilization (or control) ordinances -laws in some communities that limit or prohibit rent increases, or that limit the circumstances in which a tenant can be evicted.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: rent withholding -the tenant's remedy of not paying some or all of the rent if the landlord does not x defects that make the rental unit uninhabitable within a reasonable time after the landlord receives notice of the defects from the tenant.
texts.text: rent withholding -the tenant's remedy of not paying some or all of the rent if the landlord does not x defects that make the rental unit uninhabitable within a reasonable time after the landlord receives notice of the defects from the tenant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: rental agent -see property manager.
texts.text: rental agent -see property manager.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: rental agreement -an oral or written agreement between a tenant and a landlord, made before the tenant moves in, which establishes the terms of the tenancy, such as the amount of the rent and when it is due (see lease and periodic rental agreement).
texts.text: rental agreement -an oral or written agreement between a tenant and a landlord, made before the tenant moves in, which establishes the terms of the tenancy, such as the amount of the rent and when it is due (see lease and periodic rental agreement).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: rental application form -a form that a landlord may ask a tenant to ll out prior to renting that requests information about the tenant, such as the tenant's address, telephone number, employment history, credit references, and the like.
texts.text: rental application form -a form that a landlord may ask a tenant to ll out prior to renting that requests information about the tenant, such as the tenant's address, telephone number, employment history, credit references, and the like.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: rental period -the length of time between rent payments; for example, a week or a month.
texts.text: rental period -the length of time between rent payments; for example, a week or a month.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: rental unit -an apartment, house, duplex, condominium, accessory dwelling unit (ADU), junior accessory dwelling unit, room, or other structure or part thereof that a landlord rents to a tenant to live in.
texts.text: rental unit -an apartment, house, duplex, condominium, accessory dwelling unit (ADU), junior accessory dwelling unit, room, or other structure or part thereof that a landlord rents to a tenant to live in.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: renter's insurance -insurance protecting the tenant against property losses, such as losses from theft or re. Also, this insurance may protect the tenant against liability (legal responsibility) for claims or lawsuits led by the landlord or by others alleging that the tenant negligently injured another person or property.
texts.text: renter's insurance -insurance protecting the tenant against property losses, such as losses from theft or re. Also, this insurance may protect the tenant against liability (legal responsibility) for claims or lawsuits led by the landlord or by others alleging that the tenant negligently injured another person or property.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: repair and deduct remedy -the tenant's remedy of deducting from future rent the amount necessary to repair defects covered by the implied warranty of habitability. The amount deducted cannot be more than one month's rent.
texts.text: repair and deduct remedy -the tenant's remedy of deducting from future rent the amount necessary to repair defects covered by the implied warranty of habitability. The amount deducted cannot be more than one month's rent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: retaliatory eviction or action -an act by a landlord, such as raising a tenant's rent, seeking to evict a tenant, or otherwise punishing a tenant because the tenant has exercised a lawful right, such as using the repair and deduct remedy or the rent withholding remedy.
texts.text: retaliatory eviction or action -an act by a landlord, such as raising a tenant's rent, seeking to evict a tenant, or otherwise punishing a tenant because the tenant has exercised a lawful right, such as using the repair and deduct remedy or the rent withholding remedy.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: reusable tenant screening report -a consumer report that was prepared by a consumer reporting agency within the last 30 days at the request and expense of a rental applicant and which made available to a landlord at no cost to use in the rental application process.
texts.text: reusable tenant screening report -a consumer report that was prepared by a consumer reporting agency within the last 30 days at the request and expense of a rental applicant and which made available to a landlord at no cost to use in the rental application process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: security deposit -a deposit or a fee that the landlord requires the tenant to pay at the beginning of the tenancy. The landlord can use the security deposit, for example, if the tenant moves out owing rent or leaves the unit damaged, other than normal wear and tear, or less clean than when the tenant moved in.
texts.text: security deposit -a deposit or a fee that the landlord requires the tenant to pay at the beginning of the tenancy. The landlord can use the security deposit, for example, if the tenant moves out owing rent or leaves the unit damaged, other than normal wear and tear, or less clean than when the tenant moved in.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: serve/service -legal requirements and procedures that seek to assure that the person to whom a legal notice is directed actually receives it.
texts.text: serve/service -legal requirements and procedures that seek to assure that the person to whom a legal notice is directed actually receives it.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: sublease -a separate rental agreement between the original tenant and a new tenant to whom the original tenant rents all or part of the rental unit. The new tenant is called a 'subtenant.' The agreement between the original tenant and the landlord remains in force, and the original tenant continues to be responsible for paying the rent to the landlord and for other tenant obligations. (Compare to assignment.)
texts.text: sublease -a separate rental agreement between the original tenant and a new tenant to whom the original tenant rents all or part of the rental unit. The new tenant is called a 'subtenant.' The agreement between the original tenant and the landlord remains in force, and the original tenant continues to be responsible for paying the rent to the landlord and for other tenant obligations. (Compare to assignment.)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: subpoena -an order from the court that requires the recipient to appear as a witness or provide evidence in a court proceeding.
texts.text: subpoena -an order from the court that requires the recipient to appear as a witness or provide evidence in a court proceeding.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: subtenant -see sublease.
texts.text: subtenant -see sublease.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: tenancy -the tenant's exclusive right, created by a rental agreement between the landlord and the tenant, to use and possess the landlord's rental unit.
texts.text: tenancy -the tenant's exclusive right, created by a rental agreement between the landlord and the tenant, to use and possess the landlord's rental unit.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: tenant -a person who rents a rental unit from a landlord. The tenant obtains the right to the exclusive use and possession of the rental unit during the rental period. If the rental agreement identies more than one person as the tenant, then all of the identied people are co-tenants and together they have the right to the exclusive use and possession of the rental unit vis a vis the landlord and other third parties during the rental period but non-exclusive use and possession of the rental unit with respect to each other.
texts.text: tenant -a person who rents a rental unit from a landlord. The tenant obtains the right to the exclusive use and possession of the rental unit during the rental period. If the rental agreement identies more than one person as the tenant, then all of the identied people are co-tenants and together they have the right to the exclusive use and possession of the rental unit vis a vis the landlord and other third parties during the rental period but non-exclusive use and possession of the rental unit with respect to each other.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Tenant Protection Act of 2019 (the Tenant Protection Act) -the Tenant Protection Act imposes statewide limits on rental increases. The Tenant Protection Act also imposes statewide just cause eviction requirements for rental units where the tenant has resided at the unit for more than 12 months or 24 months if an adult tenant was added to the tenancy during the preceding 12 months.
texts.text: Tenant Protection Act of 2019 (the Tenant Protection Act) -the Tenant Protection Act imposes statewide limits on rental increases. The Tenant Protection Act also imposes statewide just cause eviction requirements for rental units where the tenant has resided at the unit for more than 12 months or 24 months if an adult tenant was added to the tenancy during the preceding 12 months.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: tenant relocation payment -a payment from the landlord to the tenant to assist the tenant with relocation if the landlord relies on no-fault just cause as the basis to terminate the tenancy and evict the tenant. Under the Tenant Protection Act of 2019, the relocation payment is an amount equal to one month of the tenant's rent that was in effect when the owner issued the notice to terminate the tenancy and, at the landlord's option, the landlord may pay this amount directly to the tenant or waive the tenant's obligation to pay his/her nal month's rent.
texts.text: tenant relocation payment -a payment from the landlord to the tenant to assist the tenant with relocation if the landlord relies on no-fault just cause as the basis to terminate the tenancy and evict the tenant. Under the Tenant Protection Act of 2019, the relocation payment is an amount equal to one month of the tenant's rent that was in effect when the owner issued the notice to terminate the tenancy and, at the landlord's option, the landlord may pay this amount directly to the tenant or waive the tenant's obligation to pay his/her nal month's rent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: tenant screening service -a credit reporting agency that collects and sells information on tenants, such as whether they paid their rent on time, whether they damaged previous rental units, whether they were the subject of an unlawful detainer lawsuit, and whether landlords considered them good or bad tenants.
texts.text: tenant screening service -a credit reporting agency that collects and sells information on tenants, such as whether they paid their rent on time, whether they damaged previous rental units, whether they were the subject of an unlawful detainer lawsuit, and whether landlords considered them good or bad tenants.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: uninhabitable -a rental unit which has such serious problems or defects that the tenant's health or safety is affected. A rental unit may be uninhabitable if it is not t for human beings to live in, if it fails to substantially comply with building and safety code standards that materially affect tenants' health and safety, if it contains a lead hazard, or
texts.text: uninhabitable -a rental unit which has such serious problems or defects that the tenant's health or safety is affected. A rental unit may be uninhabitable if it is not t for human beings to live in, if it fails to substantially comply with building and safety code standards that materially affect tenants' health and safety, if it contains a lead hazard, or

texts.label: text
texts.orig: if it is a dangerous substandard building. (Compare to habitable.)
texts.text: if it is a dangerous substandard building. (Compare to habitable.)

texts.orig: unlawful detainer lawsuit -a lawsuit that a landlord must le and win before they can evict a tenant (also called an eviction lawsuit).
texts.text: unlawful detainer lawsuit -a lawsuit that a landlord must le and win before they can evict a tenant (also called an eviction lawsuit).

texts.orig: waive/waiver -to sign a written document (a waiver) giving up a right, claim, privilege, etc. In order for a waiver to be effective, the person giving the waiver must do so knowingly, and must know the right, claim, privilege, etc. that they are giving up.
texts.text: waive/waiver -to sign a written document (a waiver) giving up a right, claim, privilege, etc. In order for a waiver to be effective, the person giving the waiver must do so knowingly, and must know the right, claim, privilege, etc. that they are giving up.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: writ of possession -a document issued by the court after the landlord wins an unlawful detainer (eviction) lawsuit. The writ of possession is served on the tenant by the sheriff. The writ informs the tenant that the tenant must leave the rental unit by the end of ve days, or the sheriff will forcibly remove the tenant.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: APPENDIX 1 - OCCUPANTS NOT NAMED IN EVICTION LAWSUIT OR WRIT OF POSSESSION
texts.text: APPENDIX 1 - OCCUPANTS NOT NAMED IN EVICTION LAWSUIT OR WRIT OF POSSESSION

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: OCCUPANTS NOT NAMED IN EVICTION LAWSUIT
texts.text: OCCUPANTS NOT NAMED IN EVICTION LAWSUIT

texts.label: text
texts.orig: People who are not named as tenants in the rental agreement sometimes move into a rental unit before the landlord les the unlawful detainer (eviction) lawsuit. The landlord may not know that these people
texts.text: People who are not named as tenants in the rental agreement sometimes move into a rental unit before the landlord les the unlawful detainer (eviction) lawsuit. The landlord may not know that these people

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (called 'occupants') are living in the rental unit, and therefore may not name them as defendants in the summons and complaint. As a result, these occupants are not named in the writ of possession if the landlord wins the unlawful detainer action. A sheriff enforcing the writ of possession cannot lawfully evict an occupant whose name does not appear on the writ of possession and who claims to have lived in the unit since before the unlawful detainer lawsuit was led, if the occupant takes the correct steps in time (see writ of possession, page 106).
texts.text: (called 'occupants') are living in the rental unit, and therefore may not name them as defendants in the summons and complaint. As a result, these occupants are not named in the writ of possession if the landlord wins the unlawful detainer action. A sheriff enforcing the writ of possession cannot lawfully evict an occupant whose name does not appear on the writ of possession and who claims to have lived in the unit since before the unlawful detainer lawsuit was led, if the occupant takes the correct steps in time (see writ of possession, page 106).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The landlord can take steps to avoid this result. The landlord can instruct the marshal, sheriff or registered process server who serves the summons and complaint on the named defendants to ask whether there are other occupants living in the unit who have not been named as defendants. If there are, the person serving the summons and complaint can serve each of the so-called 'unnamed occupants' with a blank Prejudgment Claim of Right to Possession form and an extra copy of the summons and complaint.[483]
texts.text: The landlord can take steps to avoid this result. The landlord can instruct the marshal, sheriff or registered process server who serves the summons and complaint on the named defendants to ask whether there are other occupants living in the unit who have not been named as defendants. If there are, the person serving the summons and complaint can serve each of the so-called 'unnamed occupants' with a blank Prejudgment Claim of Right to Possession form and an extra copy of the summons and complaint.[483]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: These occupants then have 10 days from the date they are served to le a Prejudgment Claim of Right to Possession form with the Clerk of Court, and to pay the clerk the required ling fee (or le an Application for Waiver of Court Fees and Costs if they are unable to pay the ling fee [see page 99]). Any unnamed occupant who does not le a Prejudgment Claim of Right to Possession form with the Clerk of Court (along with the ling fee or a request for waiver of the fee) can then be evicted if the tenants named in the eviction action lose at court.
texts.text: These occupants then have 10 days from the date they are served to le a Prejudgment Claim of Right to Possession form with the Clerk of Court, and to pay the clerk the required ling fee (or le an Application for Waiver of Court Fees and Costs if they are unable to pay the ling fee [see page 99]). Any unnamed occupant who does not le a Prejudgment Claim of Right to Possession form with the Clerk of Court (along with the ling fee or a request for waiver of the fee) can then be evicted if the tenants named in the eviction action lose at court.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An unnamed occupant who les a Prejudgment Claim of Right to Possession form automatically becomes a defendant in the unlawful detainer lawsuit and must le an answer to the complaint within ve days after ling the form.[484 ] If the landlord wins, the occupant will be subject to the eviction. Any other occupant who did not le a Prejudgment Claim cannot delay the eviction, whether or not that occupant is named in the writ of possession issued by the court.[485 ]
texts.text: An unnamed occupant who les a Prejudgment Claim of Right to Possession form automatically becomes a defendant in the unlawful detainer lawsuit and must le an answer to the complaint within ve days after ling the form.[484 ] If the landlord wins, the occupant will be subject to the eviction. Any other occupant who did not le a Prejudgment Claim cannot delay the eviction, whether or not that occupant is named in the writ of possession issued by the court.[485 ]

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: OCCUPANTS NOT NAMED IN WRIT OF POSSESSION
texts.text: OCCUPANTS NOT NAMED IN WRIT OF POSSESSION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The landlord sometimes does not serve a Prejudgment Claim of Right to Possession form on the unnamed occupants when the unlawful detainer complaint is served. When the sheriff arrives to enforce the writ of possession (that is, to evict the tenants [see writ of possession, page 106]), an occupant whose name does not appear on the writ of possession, and who claims a right of possession, may ll out a Claim of Right to Possession form and give it to the sheriff. The sheriff must then stop the eviction of that occupant and must give the occupant a copy of the completed form or a receipt for it. [486 ]
texts.text: The landlord sometimes does not serve a Prejudgment Claim of Right to Possession form on the unnamed occupants when the unlawful detainer complaint is served. When the sheriff arrives to enforce the writ of possession (that is, to evict the tenants [see writ of possession, page 106]), an occupant whose name does not appear on the writ of possession, and who claims a right of possession, may ll out a Claim of Right to Possession form and give it to the sheriff. The sheriff must then stop the eviction of that occupant and must give the occupant a copy of the completed form or a receipt for it. [486 ]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Within two court days after completing the form and giving it to the sheriff, the occupant must deliver to the Clerk of Court the court's ling fee (or le an Application for
texts.text: Within two court days after completing the form and giving it to the sheriff, the occupant must deliver to the Clerk of Court the court's ling fee (or le an Application for

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Waiver of Court Fees and Costs if the occupant is unable to pay the ling fee (see page 99)). The occupant also should deliver to the court an amount equal to 15 days' rent for the rental unit (the writ of possession must state the daily rental value of the rental unit).
texts.text: Waiver of Court Fees and Costs if the occupant is unable to pay the ling fee (see page 99)). The occupant also should deliver to the court an amount equal to 15 days' rent for the rental unit (the writ of possession must state the daily rental value of the rental unit).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Five to 15 days after the occupant has paid the ling fee (or has led a request for waiver of the fee) and has deposited an amount equal to 15 days' rent, the court will hold a hearing. If the occupant does not deposit the 15 days' rent, the court will hold the hearing within ve days .
texts.text: Five to 15 days after the occupant has paid the ling fee (or has led a request for waiver of the fee) and has deposited an amount equal to 15 days' rent, the court will hold a hearing. If the occupant does not deposit the 15 days' rent, the court will hold the hearing within ve days .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: At the hearing, the court will decide whether or not the occupant has a valid claim to possession. If the court decides that the occupant's claim to possession is valid, the amount of rent deposited will be returned to the occupant. The court will then order further proceedings, as appropriate to the case (for example, the occupant may be given ve days to answer the landlord's complaint and defend the eviction action).
texts.text: At the hearing, the court will decide whether or not the occupant has a valid claim to possession. If the court decides that the occupant's claim to possession is valid, the amount of rent deposited will be returned to the occupant. The court will then order further proceedings, as appropriate to the case (for example, the occupant may be given ve days to answer the landlord's complaint and defend the eviction action).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If the court nds that the occupant's claim to possession is not valid, an amount equal to the daily rent for each day the eviction was delayed will be subtracted from the rent that is returned to the occupant, and the sheriff or marshal will continue with the eviction.[487 ]
texts.text: If the court nds that the occupant's claim to possession is not valid, an amount equal to the daily rent for each day the eviction was delayed will be subtracted from the rent that is returned to the occupant, and the sheriff or marshal will continue with the eviction.[487 ]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Notice: The California Department of Justice, sheriff's departments, police departments serving jurisdictions of 200,000 or more, and many other local law enforcement authorities maintain for public access a database of the locations of persons required to register pursuant to paragraph (1) of subdivision (a) of Section 290.4 of the Penal Code. The database is updated on a quarterly basis and is a source of information about the presence of these individuals in any neighborhood. The Department of Justice also maintains a Sex Offender Identication Line through which inquiries about individuals may be made. This is a '900' telephone service. Callers must have specic information about individuals they are checking. Information regarding neighborhoods is not available through the '900' telephone service.

texts.orig: Lead Warning Statement
texts.text: Lead Warning Statement

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Housing built before 1978 may contain lead-based paint. Lead from paint, paint chips, and dust can pose health hazards if not managed properly. Lead exposure is especially harmful to young children and pregnant women. Before renting pre-1978 housing, lessors must disclose the presence of lead-based paint and/or lead-based paint hazards in the dwelling. Lessees must also receive a federally approved pamphlet on lead poisoning prevention.
texts.text: Housing built before 1978 may contain lead-based paint. Lead from paint, paint chips, and dust can pose health hazards if not managed properly. Lead exposure is especially harmful to young children and pregnant women. Before renting pre-1978 housing, lessors must disclose the presence of lead-based paint and/or lead-based paint hazards in the dwelling. Lessees must also receive a federally approved pamphlet on lead poisoning prevention.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (h)(2) The landlord shall also include, along with and at the time the itemized statement is sent, copies of documents showing charges incurred and deducted by the landlord to repair or clean the premises, as follows:
texts.text: (h)(2) The landlord shall also include, along with and at the time the itemized statement is sent, copies of documents showing charges incurred and deducted by the landlord to repair or clean the premises, as follows:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (A) If the landlord or landlord's employee did the work, the itemized statement shall reasonably describe the work performed. The itemized statement shall include the time spent and the reasonable hourly rate charged.
texts.text: (A) If the landlord or landlord's employee did the work, the itemized statement shall reasonably describe the work performed. The itemized statement shall include the time spent and the reasonable hourly rate charged.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (B) If the landlord or landlord's employee did not do the work, the landlord shall provide the tenant a copy of the bill, invoice, or receipt supplied by the person or entity performing the work. The itemized statement shall provide the tenant with the name, address, and telephone number of the person or entity, if the bill, invoice, or receipt does not include that information.
texts.text: (B) If the landlord or landlord's employee did not do the work, the landlord shall provide the tenant a copy of the bill, invoice, or receipt supplied by the person or entity performing the work. The itemized statement shall provide the tenant with the name, address, and telephone number of the person or entity, if the bill, invoice, or receipt does not include that information.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (C) If a deduction is made for materials or supplies, the landlord shall provide a copy of the bill, invoice, or receipt. If a particular material or supply item is purchased by the landlord on an ongoing basis, the landlord may document the cost of the item by providing a copy of a bill, invoice, receipt, vendor price list, or other vendor document that reasonably documents the cost of the item used in the repair or cleaning of the unit.
texts.text: (C) If a deduction is made for materials or supplies, the landlord shall provide a copy of the bill, invoice, or receipt. If a particular material or supply item is purchased by the landlord on an ongoing basis, the landlord may document the cost of the item by providing a copy of a bill, invoice, receipt, vendor price list, or other vendor document that reasonably documents the cost of the item used in the repair or cleaning of the unit.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (D) If a deduction is made for repairs or cleanings allowed by this section, the landlord shall provide photographs taken pursuant to subdivision (g), along with a written explanation of the cost of the allowable repairs or cleanings, as described in subparagraphs (A) to (C), inclusive. The landlord may provide such photographs to the tenant by mail, email, computer ash drive, or by providing a link where the tenant may view the photographs online.
texts.text: (D) If a deduction is made for repairs or cleanings allowed by this section, the landlord shall provide photographs taken pursuant to subdivision (g), along with a written explanation of the cost of the allowable repairs or cleanings, as described in subparagraphs (A) to (C), inclusive. The landlord may provide such photographs to the tenant by mail, email, computer ash drive, or by providing a link where the tenant may view the photographs online.

texts.text: Civil Code Sections 1950.5(b)(1)-(4) (as of January 1, 2011). (This text of the security deposit statute must accompany the landlord's itemized statement of repairs or cleaning.)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (b) As used in this section, 'security' means any payment, fee, deposit or charge, including, but not limited to, any payment, fee, deposit, or charge, except as provided in Section 1950.6, that is imposed at the beginning of the tenancy to be used to reimburse the landlord for costs associated with processing a new tenant or that is imposed as an advance payment of rent, used or to be used for any purpose, including, but not limited to, any of the following:
texts.text: (b) As used in this section, 'security' means any payment, fee, deposit or charge, including, but not limited to, any payment, fee, deposit, or charge, except as provided in Section 1950.6, that is imposed at the beginning of the tenancy to be used to reimburse the landlord for costs associated with processing a new tenant or that is imposed as an advance payment of rent, used or to be used for any purpose, including, but not limited to, any of the following:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (1) The compensation of a landlord for a tenant's default in the payment of rent.
texts.text: The compensation of a landlord for a tenant's default in the payment of rent.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (2) The repair of damages to the premises, exclusive of ordinary wear and tear, caused by the tenant or by a guest or licensee of the tenant.
texts.text: The repair of damages to the premises, exclusive of ordinary wear and tear, caused by the tenant or by a guest or licensee of the tenant.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (3) The cleaning of the premises upon termination of the tenancy is necessary to return the unit to the same level of cleanliness it was in at the inception of the tenancy. The amendments to this paragraph enacted by the act adding this sentence shall apply only to tenancies for which the tenant's right to occupy begins after January 1, 2003.
texts.text: The cleaning of the premises upon termination of the tenancy is necessary to return the unit to the same level of cleanliness it was in at the inception of the tenancy. The amendments to this paragraph enacted by the act adding this sentence shall apply only to tenancies for which the tenant's right to occupy begins after January 1, 2003.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (4) To remedy future defaults by the tenant in any obligation under the rental
texts.text: To remedy future defaults by the tenant in any obligation under the rental

texts.label: text
texts.orig: agreement to restore, replace, or return personal property or appurtenances, exclusive of ordinary wear and tear, if the security deposit is authorized to be applied thereto by the rental agreement.
texts.text: agreement to restore, replace, or return personal property or appurtenances, exclusive of ordinary wear and tear, if the security deposit is authorized to be applied thereto by the rental agreement.

texts.text: CONDOMINIUM CONVERSION NOTICE (see page 67)

texts.orig: Government Code Section 66459. (This notice must be printed in at least 14-point bold type.)
texts.text: Government Code Section 66459. (This notice must be printed in at least 14-point bold type.)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: TO THE PROSPECTIVE TENANTS OF: ADDRESS:
texts.text: TO THE PROSPECTIVE TENANTS OF: ADDRESS:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: THE UNIT YOU MAY RENT HAS BEEN APPROVED FOR SALE TO THE PUBLIC AS A CONDOMINIUM PROJECT, COMMUNITY APARTMENT PROJECT, OR STOCK COOPERATIVE PROJECT (WHICHEVER APPLIES). THE RENTAL UNIT MAY BE SOLD TO THE PUBLIC, AND, IF IT IS OFFERED FOR SALE, YOUR LEASE MAY BE TERMINATED. YOU WILL BE NOTIFIED AT LEAST 90 DAYS PRIOR TO ANY OFFERING TO SELL. IF YOU STILL LAWFULLY RESIDE IN THE UNIT, YOU WILL BE GIVEN A RIGHT OF FIRST REFUSAL TO PURCHASE THE UNIT.
texts.text: THE UNIT YOU MAY RENT HAS BEEN APPROVED FOR SALE TO THE PUBLIC AS A CONDOMINIUM PROJECT, COMMUNITY APARTMENT PROJECT, OR STOCK COOPERATIVE PROJECT (WHICHEVER APPLIES). THE RENTAL UNIT MAY BE SOLD TO THE PUBLIC, AND, IF IT IS OFFERED FOR SALE, YOUR LEASE MAY BE TERMINATED. YOU WILL BE NOTIFIED AT LEAST 90 DAYS PRIOR TO ANY OFFERING TO SELL. IF YOU STILL LAWFULLY RESIDE IN THE UNIT, YOU WILL BE GIVEN A RIGHT OF FIRST REFUSAL TO PURCHASE THE UNIT.

texts.text: SIGNATURE OF OWNER OR OWNER'S AGENT: DATED:

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: RENT CAP AND STATEMENT OF CAUSE TO TERMINATE TENANCY NOTICE
texts.text: RENT CAP AND STATEMENT OF CAUSE TO TERMINATE TENANCY NOTICE

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Civil Code Section 1946.2 (This notice must be printed in at least 12-point font.)
texts.text: Civil Code Section 1946.2 (This notice must be printed in at least 12-point font.)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 'California law limits the amount your rent can be increased. See Section 1947.12 of the Civil Code for more information. California law also provides that after all of the tenants have continuously and lawfully occupied the property for 12 months or more or at least one of the tenants has continuously and lawfully occupied the property for 24 months or more, a landlord must provide a statement of cause in any notice to terminate a tenancy. See Section 1946.2 of the Civil Code for more information.'
texts.text: 'California law limits the amount your rent can be increased. See Section 1947.12 of the Civil Code for more information. California law also provides that after all of the tenants have continuously and lawfully occupied the property for 12 months or more or at least one of the tenants has continuously and lawfully occupied the property for 24 months or more, a landlord must provide a statement of cause in any notice to terminate a tenancy. See Section 1946.2 of the Civil Code for more information.'
